    Filamentary Star Formation

    Filamentary Star Formation

    Type:Intermediate concept
    Typical filament width:~0.1 pc (Herschel)
    Key paradigm:Network → filament → core → star
    Related: Molecular clouds
    B-fields in star formation
    IRDCs

    Filamentary star formation refers to the paradigm — established through Herschel observations — that star formation proceeds through a hierarchical pathway: the ISM organizes into filamentary networks, within which dense cores condense along supercritical filaments and collapse to form stars[1]. Filaments are ubiquitous structures observed at all scales in molecular clouds[2].

    Filament Properties

    Herschel observations revealed that interstellar filaments have a characteristic width of ~0.1 pc across a wide range of environments[2], though this universality is debated. Filament radial profiles are well-described by Plummer-like functions with a flattening radius R_flat[1]. Tools such as RadFil are used to identify filament spines and extract profiles[3].

    Filaments host embedded clumps and cores identified by surveys like ATLASGAL[4]. In G351.77-0.53, the filament extends ~7.8 pc with a flattening radius of ~0.55 pc and total mass ~4150 M☉[5].

    Hub-Filament Systems

    Hub-filament systems (HFS) are configurations where multiple filaments converge on a central dense hub — the sites of the most massive star formation[6]. Material flows along filaments toward the hub, channeled by magnetic fields that align with the low-density striations but are perpendicular to the dense spine[7].

    In G351.77-0.53, multiple sub-filaments converge toward the central clump c1, potentially contributing to the observed hourglass B-field morphology through their alignment with the field[8][5].

    Magnetic Fields and Filaments

    Magnetic fields are intimately linked to filament dynamics. Planck observations show B-fields perpendicular to dense filaments, consistent with matter flowing along field lines to build up the filament[9]. MHD simulations confirm that turbulence and B-field orientation significantly impact filament structure and fragmentation[10][11].

    See Also

    • Molecular clouds
    • Infrared dark clouds
    • Magnetic fields in star formation
    • Hourglass B-field morphology
    • Jadhav et al. (2025)

    References
    1. André, Ph., et al. (2014). "From Filamentary Networks to Dense Cores." PP VI, 27. DOI:10.2458/azu_uapress_9780816531240-ch002
    2. Arzoumanian, D., et al. (2011). "Filaments in IC 5146." A&A, 529, L6. DOI:10.1051/0004-6361/201116596
    3. Zucker, C., et al. (2018). "RadFil: filament profile extraction." ApJ, 864, 152. DOI:10.3847/1538-4357/aad3b5
    4. Urquhart, J. S., et al. (2017). "ATLASGAL clumps." MNRAS, 473, 1059. DOI:10.1093/mnras/stx2258
    5. Jadhav, O. R., et al. (2025). "Hourglass B-field toward IRDC G351.77-0.53." arXiv:2603.16425
    6. Myers, P. C. (2009). "Filamentary Structure of Star-Forming Complexes." ApJ, 700, 1609. DOI:10.1088/0004-637X/700/2/1609
    7. Pillai, T. G. S., et al. (2020). "Magnetized gas flows in Serpens South." Nature Astron., 4, 1195. DOI:10.1038/s41550-020-1172-6
    8. Leurini, S., et al. (2019). "Sub-filaments in G351." A&A, 621, A130. DOI:10.1051/0004-6361/201833612
    9. Planck Collaboration (2016). "B-fields in filaments." A&A, 586, A138. DOI:10.1051/0004-6361/201525896
    10. Seifried, D., et al. (2015). "Turbulence and B-fields in filaments." MNRAS, 452, 2410. DOI:10.1093/mnras/stv1458
    11. Chen, C.-Y. & Ostriker, E. C. (2015). "Magnetized core formation." ApJ, 810, 126. DOI:10.1088/0004-637X/810/2/126
